entries.statement: Knowledge of supervised and unsupervised machine learning algorithms, including their mathematical foundations, hyperparameter behavior, and appropriate application conditions.
Knowledge of statistical inference methods, including hypothesis testing, confidence intervals, Bayesian inference, and the assumptions underlying each method.

Knowledge of feature engineering techniques, including encoding strategies, normalization, handling of missing data, feature selection methods, and the relationship between feature quality and model performance.
Knowledge of ML model serving patterns, including REST API serving, batch inference, streaming inference, edge deployment, and the latency, throughput, and cost trade-offs of each.
Knowledge of containerization and orchestration technologies (Docker, Kubernetes) as applied to ML model packaging, deployment, and scaling.
Knowledge of ML pipeline frameworks and the engineering practices required to make training and inference pipelines reproducible, testable, and maintainable.
Knowledge of model monitoring approaches, including prediction drift detection, input data distribution monitoring, and latency and throughput tracking.
Knowledge of large language model capabilities, limitations, failure modes, and the prompt engineering and system design patterns that shape reliable LLM-based application behavior.
Knowledge of retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) architecture patterns, including vector store design, chunking strategies, retrieval evaluation, and hallucination mitigation approaches.
Knowledge of agentic AI design patterns, including tool use, multi-agent coordination, memory systems, and the safety and reliability challenges specific to agentic architectures.

Knowledge of NLP task taxonomy, including classification, named entity recognition, relation extraction, summarization, translation, and question answering, and the model architectures appropriate for each.
Knowledge of transformer architecture, attention mechanisms, and the pre-training and fine-tuning paradigm as it applies to language models.
Knowledge of computer vision task taxonomy, including image classification, object detection, segmentation, pose estimation, and generation, and the architectures appropriate for each.
Knowledge of CNN architectures, vision transformer architectures, and the data augmentation, regularization, and transfer learning techniques applicable to visual recognition tasks.
Knowledge of cloud data platform services across major providers, including storage, compute, streaming, and orchestration capabilities and their architectural implications.
Knowledge of ML platform components including feature stores, model registries, experiment tracking systems, model serving infrastructure, and pipeline orchestration frameworks.
Knowledge of MLOps principles and practices, including CI/CD for ML, automated retraining pipelines, model versioning, A/B testing infrastructure, and canary deployment patterns.
Knowledge of cloud AI/ML platform services (e.g., SageMaker, Vertex AI, Azure ML) and the architectural patterns for building portable versus cloud-native ML infrastructure.
Knowledge of GPU and accelerated compute infrastructure, including cluster management, resource scheduling, distributed training architectures, and cost optimization strategies.
Knowledge of data storage technologies, including columnar storage formats (Parquet, ORC), transactional databases, NoSQL stores, object storage, and their appropriate use cases.

Knowledge of cloud data platform infrastructure services, including managed compute, serverless processing, object storage, data warehouse, and streaming platform options across major cloud providers.

Knowledge of ML pipeline orchestration tools and patterns, including feature stores, experiment tracking, model registries, and the architectural trade-offs between managed and self-hosted ML platforms.

Knowledge of model deployment strategies, including batch inference, real-time serving, edge deployment, A/B testing, canary releases, and shadow deployments, and the infrastructure requirements and failure modes of each.

Knowledge of model monitoring requirements, including data drift detection, prediction drift, performance degradation metrics, and the alerting thresholds and retraining triggers appropriate for different model types and serving patterns.

Knowledge of containerization and infrastructure-as-code as applied to ML workloads, including GPU resource management, distributed training job scheduling, and reproducible environment management across development and production.

Knowledge of DataOps principles and practices, including pipeline automation, environment management, data testing strategies, configuration-as-code, and the distinction between DataOps, DevOps, and MLOps as complementary disciplines.
Knowledge of current frontiers in machine learning and AI research, including recent advances in foundation models, reinforcement learning, causal ML, and AI safety research.
Knowledge of experimental design for ML research, including ablation study design, statistical significance in ML evaluation, benchmark construction, and reproducibility standards.
Knowledge of AI capability landscape, including current capabilities and limitations of generative AI, predictive AI, and autonomous systems as applied to enterprise business problems.

Knowledge of AI risk management frameworks and safety engineering principles, including risk identification across the AI lifecycle, risk categorization methodologies, and the design of mitigation measures that balance safety with system performance.

Knowledge of AI ethics principles and fairness evaluation methods, including bias detection and measurement techniques, fairness metrics, representativeness assessment, and the relationship between training data characteristics and model equity outcomes.

Knowledge of AI governance and regulatory compliance requirements, including the EU AI Act risk classification system, NIST AI RMF governance functions, organizational AI policy design, and conformity assessment procedures.

Knowledge of AI security threats and adversarial ML techniques, including data poisoning, model extraction, evasion attacks, prompt injection, and the defensive measures and robustness testing approaches applicable to each.

Knowledge of human-AI interaction design principles, including human oversight mechanisms, human-in-the-loop and human-on-the-loop architectures, interpretability requirements for different decision contexts, and the design of effective AI system transparency.

Knowledge of AI supply chain risk and third-party model dependencies, including pre-trained model provenance assessment, API dependency management, intellectual property considerations, and contingency planning for third-party AI service disruption.

Knowledge of AI system lifecycle management, including inception assessment, development governance, deployment readiness evaluation, post-market monitoring, substantial modification criteria, and decommissioning procedures.

Knowledge of AI testing, evaluation, verification, and validation (TEVV) methodology, including test protocol design for high-risk systems, real-world testing requirements, red-teaming approaches, and the assessment of TEVV process effectiveness.

Knowledge of AI data governance requirements specific to ML systems, including training data provenance, data quality standards for model inputs, annotation governance, synthetic data generation principles, and data lifecycle management across model versions.

Knowledge of AI business value assessment methods, including use case identification and prioritization, feasibility evaluation, build-versus-buy analysis, ROI estimation for AI initiatives, and the translation of business problems into technically feasible AI solutions.

Knowledge of multimodal AI systems, including architectures that process and integrate across text, image, audio, and video modalities, cross-modal alignment techniques, and the evaluation challenges specific to multimodal outputs.
